Document [1] (Title: Cerebral Spinal Fluid Leak Disorders (Archived) -- Treatment / Management -- CSF Diversion Strategies) If conservative strategies fail, continuous or intermittent CSF drainage using a lumbar drain is performed. Continuous drainage is usually done at the rate of 5 ml/h to 10 ml/h (150 to 200 ml/day), while for intermittent drainage protocol, 20 ml to 30 ml of CSF is drained every 4 hours. Caution should be maintained to prevent over-drainage. A trial of CSF diversion is generally continued for 5 to 7 days—failure in control of the CSF leak warrants surgical management. In the scenario where conservative measures fail, surgical management is indicated irrespective of the leak's etiology. The surgical procedure and approach are tailored depending on the site of the leak.
Document [2] (Title: Cerebrospinal Fluid Leak -- Epidemiology) A CSF leak is a known complication of posttraumatic surgical procedures, endoscopic endonasal skull base surgery, operations involving the lumbosacral spine, and diagnostic or therapeutic lumbar punctures. The incidence of CSF leaks after primary spine surgery ranges from 5.5% to 9%, and from 13.2% to 21% after the second surgery. [33] Nonidentified intraoperative durotomies occurred in 6.8% of cases, and the incidence of CSF leaks was less frequent in minimally invasive surgeries (4.7%) compared to 9.0% for open surgical cases, according to Wong et al. [34] A recent literature review of CSF leaks in the endoscopic endonasal approach for tumor resection showed an overall postoperative CSF leak rate of 10.1%. Furthermore, the material used for the closure and the surgical technique can influence the leakage rate. [35]
Document [3] (Title: Cerebrospinal Fluid Leak -- Treatment / Management) The lumbar drain placement procedure involves inserting a catheter under local anesthesia into the lumbar intrathecal space to temporarily relieve symptoms and allow the CSF leak time to resolve. The CSF can then be removed from the body by connecting the catheter to a drainage system. The size and location of the leak will determine how long the catheter must remain in place. The patient often stays in the hospital for a few days following the procedure for observation and to receive any necessary follow-up care. [52] Surgery is recommended when the leak site has been identified, severe symptoms persist, and are refractory to less invasive treatments.

A meticulous fascial closure in layers is paramount in achieving closure of the spinal CSF fistula.
Document [5] (Title: Cerebral Spinal Fluid Leak Disorders (Archived) -- Treatment / Management) For persistent leaks, drainage of CSF via repeated lumbar puncture or continuous lumbar drainage via percutaneous catheter can be done. However, for the non-traumatic causes or iatrogenic trauma during surgery, the likelihood of resolution with conservative management is lower. Nonetheless, an initial trial of conservative management followed by CSF diversion is instituted. Iatrogenic CSF leaks during posterior fossa surgeries can be prevented by meticulous watertight dural closure and obliteration of air cells with bone wax or by using a muscle patch with fibrin glue.

Document [8] (Title: Cerebral Spinal Fluid Leak Disorders (Archived) -- Treatment / Management -- Timing of Surgery) The decision to proceed with definitive surgical management depends on the etiology and presentation concerning the initial insult. Early surgery without trial of conservative management or CSF diversion is indicated in patients suffering from penetrating injuries like gunshot wounds, meningitis, pneumocephalus, large identifiable bony defect, and external herniation of brain parenchyma through the wound. An initial trial of conservative management followed by CSF diversion is instituted in the remaining cases, and delayed definitive surgical management is planned if the above measures fail.

Document [10] (Title: Cerebral Spinal Fluid Leak Disorders (Archived) -- Treatment / Management -- Cranial CSF leaks) The traditional approach is a transcranial repair of skull base defects. For defects in the anterior cranial fossa, a bicoronal scalp flap is raised, and a pedicled pericranial flap is harvested. After a bifrontal craniotomy, the leak site is identified, and a combined intra and extradural repair is performed. [20] An autologous graft like temporal fascia or fascia lata is used intradural secured with fibrin glue and non-absorbable sutures along with a pedicled pericranial flap being placed extradural at the defect site to achieve a watertight dural seal. In cases where the autologous graft is unavailable, artificial dural substitutes can be used. A surgical repair is usually unnecessary for the leaks originating in the middle and posterior fossa as the spontaneous closure rate is higher. However, a subtemporal approach is preferred for a longitudinal petrous temporal fracture if needed. For a transverse fracture, a translabyrinthine or posterior fossa approach is taken depending on hearing status, which is preserved in the latter. [21]

While epidural abscesses are serious, meningitis presents an even greater risk. Because pain pumps are placed in the subarachnoid space, clinicians should monitor for signs of meningitis and have a low threshold for performing a lumbar puncture and consulting infectious disease specialists. Temporary and permanent pain pump insertions typically require an overnight hospital stay. However, some clinicians may use a single short-acting opioid spinal injection to assess pain response, allowing discharge after 6 to 8 hours. While this approach is more straightforward and cost-effective, it does not provide the advantage of evaluating how the device affects daily functioning and quality of life over an extended period. Postimplant spinal headaches may occur due to accidental dural puncture during spinal cord stimulator placement or intentional subarachnoid catheter placement. A blood patch is often an effective treatment option for this complication. Although no formal guidelines exist, early intervention, such as placing a temporary patch the following day, may help. For permanent pain pump implants, placement of a purse-string suture around the catheter may reduce the risk of postprocedural headaches.

Document [25] (Title: Lumbar Spine Imaging -- Computed Tomography) CT is a reliable method for evaluating the lumbar spine and provides great bony detail for assessing trauma, nondisplaced fractures, complex fractures, scoliosis, pre, and postoperative patients (see Image. Grade II Spondylolisthesis, CT). CT is beneficial following surgery in evaluating the surgical hardware and bone graft material, which are not well assessed on MRI. CT myelograms are useful in patients with various spinal equipment and in patients who have a contraindication for MRI. These take place after instilling a radiopaque dye into the subarachnoid space by lumbar puncture under fluoroscopy or CT guidance. [7] CT myelogram is also important for diagnosing occult sites of CSF leak following lumbar spine surgery or trauma. [8] Contraindications for CT myelogram include pregnancy and other general contraindications for a lumbar puncture, such as severe bleeding disorders and elevated intracranial pressure.

Document [27] (Title: Cerebrospinal Fluid Leak -- Treatment / Management) Treatment of a CSF leak depends on the dural tear's underlying cause, size, and location. If the CSF leak is small, it may resolve spontaneously, but larger leaks may require surgical intervention. Conservative treatment for a CSF leak typically includes bed rest and increased oral fluid intake. Other treatments, such as a lumbar epidural blood patch, may be recommended if the leak persists. Treatments for a nasal CSF leak may include nasal packing, endoscopic repair, and surgical repair.
Document [28] (Title: Lumbar Spine Imaging -- Clinical Significance -- Postoperative Lumbar Spine Imaging) With an increasing number of spine surgeries, imaging of postoperative complications has also become very common. Complications are classified as early and late complications. Early complications include hardware malpositioning, hematoma, infection, and CSF leakage. Late complications include recurrent disc herniation, failure of fusion, hardware failure, infection (discitis, osteomyelitis, prevertebral, paraspinal, and epidural abscess), epidural fibrosis, and arachnoiditis. [16] Recurrent disc herniation is distinguishable from granulation tissue that develops following surgery. On imaging, recurrent disc enhances less avidly than granulation tissue. Disc herniation can have a rim-enhancing pattern. While hardware failure and non-fusion can be evaluated on CT, MRI is necessary to diagnose most of the other postoperative complications like epidural fibrosis and arachnoiditis.
Document [29] (Title: Cerebral Spinal Fluid Leak Disorders (Archived) -- Prognosis) Many CSF leaks, especially traumatic causes, resolve spontaneously and are associated with a good prognosis. Among the remaining patients, more than 90% resolved with surgical repair. Meningitis may complicate the clinical course in a small proportion of cases, which may worsen the prognosis, especially among the late-onset CSF leaks. Prognosis is guarded among patients needing repeated surgical procedures due to the added surgical morbidity and the additional risk of meningitis.

Document [3] (Title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 620) Exploration procedures constitute a broad category of surgical interventions that are employed to diagnose, assess, and treat pathology across multiple anatomical regions. In the endocrine system, adrenalectomy—performed partially or completely via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, with or without biopsy and occasionally combined with excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors—serves both therapeutic removal and exploratory evaluation of adrenal disease. Hepatobiliary exploration includes cholecystectomy with common duct exploration, which may be performed laparoscopically or with intra‑operative cholangiography, and choledochotomy or choledochostomy procedures that incorporate ductal drainage, calculus removal, and adjunctive transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty. Neurosurgical exploration is represented by suboccipital craniectomy for cranial nerve decompression and transcranial orbital exploration for lesion excision. Spinal exploration encompasses a spectrum of laminectomy techniques—cervical, lumbar, sacral, and thoracic—designed to decompress the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy, as well as more extensive multilevel thoracic laminectomies and laminotomies (hemilaminectomies) that include nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, and disc excision. Additional specialized explorations include nipple exploration with lactiferous duct excision, parathyroidectomy with or without mediastinal exploration via sternal split or transthoracic approaches, and laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common duct exploration. Collectively, these methods illustrate how exploration procedures are tailored to the target organ and clinical objective, often integrating diagnostic biopsy, lesion removal, drainage, or decompression to optimize patient care.
Document [4] (Title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2037) Exploration procedures encompass a broad range of operative techniques in which surgeons directly examine an anatomic region to diagnose, treat, or remove pathology. In hepatobiliary surgery, cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, with or without cholangiography or choledochoenterostomy, is classified as an exploration method, as is choledochotomy or choledochostomy performed for ductal exploration, drainage, or stone removal, sometimes accompanied by transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty. Neurosurgical exploration is represented by craniectomy or craniotomy performed for supratentorial investigation and suboccipital craniectomy aimed at exploring or decompressing cranial nerves. Spinal exploration procedures consist of laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy; these are specified for one or two vertebral segments in the cervical, lumbar (excluding spondylolisthesis), and thoracic regions, and for more than two thoracic segments, with the latter variations noted as possibly included methods. Urologic and endocrine exploration includes radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration and parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration. The broader classification “Clinical actions A‑G” serves as a parent category for exploration procedures, indicating that these diverse surgical methods are organized under a common clinical action framework. Document [7] (Title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2570) Excision is a broad surgical category that includes a wide range of operative techniques across several organ systems. It encompasses adrenalectomy procedures—both partial and complete, or exploration of the adrenal gland with or without biopsy performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, including cases with excision of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—as well as choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage, or calculus removal, which may be combined with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty. Excision also covers exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the supratentorial region, and various laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) techniques for cervical and lumbar nerve‑root decompression that may involve partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and excision of herniated intervertebral disc. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration is another method classified under Excision, along with multiple forms of parathyroidectomy—standard cervical parathyroidectomy, parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via cervical, sternal split, or transthoracic approaches, and parathyroidectomy combined with mediastinal exploration. Excision is further linked to “other or therapeutic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx” and is associated with plastic procedures on the nose, reflecting its inclusion in the broader grouping “Clinical actions A‑G.” However, certain spinal procedures are explicitly excluded from the Excision category, notably laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy (e.g., procedures for spinal stenosis involving one or two vertebral segments). This integrated overview delineates the diverse methods that fall under Excision, clarifies which procedures are excluded, and highlights its relevance for procedural classification and patient‑care planning.

Document [10] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1712) Excision serves as a broad parent category for a variety of surgical actions, including the set of procedures labeled “Clinical actions A‑G.” Within this overarching category, many specific operative methods are classified as forms of excision: cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (both open and laparoscopic), choledochotomy or choledochostomy with duct exploration, drainage or calculus removal (with or without cholecystotomy and with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty), transcranial orbital exploration with lesion removal, cervical and lumbar laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) that decompress nerve roots and may involve partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, or disc excision, radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration, and parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration performed via a cervical approach or a sternal split approach. At the same time, the excision category explicitly excludes several laminectomy techniques used for spinal stenosis: thoracic laminectomy with cord or cauda equina decompression without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for one or two vertebral segments; and cervical, lumbar, or thoracic laminectomy for more than two vertebral segments performed under the same constraints. Excision is also linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, indicating that excisional techniques extend beyond deep organ surgery to include facial and upper aerodigestive tract interventions. This integrated view highlights excision as a versatile surgical concept that encompasses numerous specific operative methods while delineating clear boundaries for procedures it does not cover.

Document [14] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1713) Exploration procedures constitute a broad category of surgical interventions that are employed to examine, diagnose, or treat pathology within various anatomical regions. Specific operations that are classified as methods of exploration include partial or complete adrenalectomy—or adrenal gland exploration with or without biopsy—performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, and may also involve excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. In hepatobiliary surgery, cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, whether accompanied by cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, or transduodenal sphincterotomy/sphincteroplasty, is regarded as an exploration method. Neurosurgical exploration is represented by both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy performed for investigative purposes, as well as suboccipital craniectomy aimed at exploring or decompressing cranial nerves. Orthopedic exploration of the spinal canal is achieved through laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy; this technique is applied to one or two vertebral segments in the cervical, lumbar (excluding spondylolisthesis), and sacral regions, and may be listed as a possibly included method in broader procedural codes. Additional exploration procedures encompass nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct, and parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration, which can be performed alone or with mediastinal exploration via sternal split or transthoracic approaches, including specific transthoracic mediastinal exploration. Collectively, these diverse surgical methods illustrate how exploration procedures are tailored to the target organ system and clinical objective, serving both diagnostic and therapeutic roles in patient care.
